title: False Positives vs. False Negatives
concept: False positives are incorrect detections where none exist, while false negatives are missed detections.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main limitation of relying solely on overall accuracy metrics?

options.text: Accuracy hides poor performance on small, critical subsets of the data
Accuracy cannot be calculated on GPUs
Accuracy requires annotations to be in XML format

exercises.explain: Overall accuracy averages all samples, masking failure loops on specific categories or scenarios.

cards.title: A double-edged sword
cards.body: Imagine a cancer detection AI. If it says a healthy patient has cancer, it causes panic. If it says a sick patient is healthy, it misses the chance to save a life. These two types of mistakes have completely different real-world consequences.

cards.title: FP vs. FN
cards.body: A False Positive (FP) occurs when a model detects an object that is not actually there (false alarm). A False Negative (FN) occurs when the model fails to detect an object that is present (missed target).

cards.title: The Smoke Alarm
cards.body: Think of a smoke alarm. A false positive is when it goes off because you burnt toast (annoying, but safe). A false negative is when a fire is burning, but the alarm remains silent (highly dangerous).

cards.title: Self-Driving Detection
cards.body: In self-driving cars, a false positive on a shadow causes sudden, dangerous phantom braking. A false negative on a pedestrian causes a catastrophic collision. Engineers tune thresholds to balance these risks.

cards.title: You cannot easily minimize both to zero
cards.body: You might think you can build a model with zero false positives and zero false negatives. In reality, adjusting the model's confidence threshold is a trade-off: raising it reduces FPs but increases FNs, and vice-versa.

left.text:
Model draws a box on empty space.
Detects a 'ghost' object.
Risk: Nuisance, false alarms.

right.text:
Model ignores a real object.
Misses the target entirely.
Risk: Hazard, danger.

exercises.prompt: What is a False Negative in computer vision?
exercises.explain: A false negative means the model missed a target that was actually in the image.

options.text: When a model fails to detect an object that is actually present
When a model draws a box around an empty background area
When a model correctly identifies an object but gets the coordinates wrong
When the input image has negative pixel values

exercises.prompt: True or False: Raising the confidence threshold of an object detector will decrease False Positives but increase False Negatives.
exercises.explain: True. Higher thresholds force the model to be strict, making fewer false alarms (less FP) but missing borderline objects (more FN).
The leaf was detected as an intruder, which is a false alarm or false positive.
exercises.sentence: If a security camera triggers a false alarm because of a blowing leaf, it is a ___.

exercises.prompt: In a medical screening application, which error type is generally considered most dangerous?
exercises.explain: Missing a disease (False Negative) delays critical treatment, posing a far higher risk than a false alarm.

exercises.prompt: You are deploying a wild animal detection camera to protect sheep. False alarms (FPs) trigger a loud siren, which annoys the neighborhood. Misses (FNs) result in lost sheep. How do you tune the threshold?

options.text: Determine the cost of a lost sheep vs. a noise complaint, and set the confidence threshold to minimize the more costly error.
Set the threshold to exactly 0.0 to guarantee all animals are detected.
Disable the threshold entirely and trigger the siren randomly.
Set the threshold to 1.0 to ensure the siren never goes off.

exercises.explain: Tuning threshold involves balancing the real-world trade-off between false alarms and missed targets based on domain costs.
